does: LatentGym introduces a controllable benchmark suite for evaluating how agents learn shared latent structure across sequences of related tasks. Each environment is constructed around a ground-truth latent variable that governs task structure, enabling precise measurement of whether agents discover and exploit this hidden commonality. The paper develops metrics that separately quantify exploration—whether agents gather information about the latent structure through their actions—and exploitation—whether they use discovered structure to improve performance. The authors use this framework to empirically investigate how frontier language models and other agents fail at cross-task adaptation, evaluate whether post-training on task sequences improves generalization, and pinpoint where adaptation gains occur or are lost. The testbed provides controlled environments where the relationship between latent structure and task variation is transparent and measurable.
connects: This work builds on long-standing research in meta-learning and transfer learning that seeks to enable agents to leverage experience on one task to improve performance on related tasks. It engages with multi-task learning literature exploring how agents infer shared structure across task families, and extends recent work on continual learning systems that must adapt online. The paper responds to limitations in existing evaluation frameworks—which lack controllable latent structure or clear metrics for measuring what aspects of cross-task learning succeed or fail. It connects to psychophysical work on human learning and adaptation, and borrows from reinforcement learning's tradition of instrumented testbeds that decompose learning into separable components. The focus on post-training approaches relates to growing efforts to improve language models' adaptation capabilities through targeted training on diverse task sequences.
enables: LatentGym opens pathways for systematic study of how agents discover and exploit task structure at scale. The controlled latent structure enables research into what inductive biases and architectural components help models infer shared task relationships, potentially informing design of better meta-learning algorithms. The separate exploration-exploitation metrics allow fine-grained diagnosis of failure modes in cross-task adaptation, pointing toward targeted interventions. This foundation could support investigation into whether continual learning on diverse task families improves general-purpose assistance systems, and how to design training regimes that build robust adaptation capability. The testbed methodology itself offers a template for creating other instrumented benchmarks around hidden variables, applicable to domains like personalization, few-shot learning, and interactive assistance where understanding why agents succeed or fail at transfer is crucial for deployment.
